summary: Mariah Carey surprises the congregation at Ebenezer Baptist Church on Easter, Atlanta Pride opens grand marshal nominations, and the Hawks gear up for a make-or-break final week. Plus: Decatur weighs historic district status for Beacon Hill, the DeKalb DA draws a line on HB 369, and the Atlanta Press Club sets its midterm debate calendar.

topStory.headline: Mariah Carey Surprises Ebenezer Baptist Church Easter Congregation
topStory.summary: The Grammy winner joined Senator Raphael Warnock's Easter Resurrection Service at one of America's most historic Black churches, drawing a packed crowd to Auburn Avenue.
topStory.body:
Mariah Carey made a surprise appearance at the Easter Sunday Resurrection Service at Ebenezer Baptist Church, the historic Auburn Avenue congregation where Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. once preached.

The service was led by the Rev. Senator Raphael Warnock, who acknowledged the Grammy-winning singer's presence and her connection to the church community. Carey was seated among the congregation for the full service, drawing attention from attendees who shared the moment on social media throughout the morning.

Ebenezer Baptist Church has long been a destination for high-profile visitors, but Easter Sunday services carry particular weight at the church. The Resurrection Service is one of the most attended events on Ebenezer's annual calendar, and Carey's appearance added an unexpected layer of excitement to the holiday.

Warnock, who has served as senior pastor at Ebenezer since 2005 while simultaneously representing Georgia in the U.S. Senate, used the Easter message to speak about renewal and community resilience. The church, which sits at the heart of the Sweet Auburn Historic District, remains one of Atlanta's most visited landmarks and a living symbol of the city's civil rights legacy.

Carey has previously spoken about her faith and its role in her life and career. Her appearance at Ebenezer placed her in a lineage of musicians, politicians, and public figures who have worshipped at the church over its more than 130-year history.
topStory.imageAlt: Mariah Carey at Ebenezer Baptist Church Easter service in Sweet Auburn

stories.headline: 404 Day Parade Rolls Through Atlanta in a Celebration of Local Culture
stories.summary: The annual 404 Day Parade brought together community organizations, artists, and residents for a march through the city celebrating all things Atlanta.
stories.body:
Atlanta marked 404 Day with its annual parade, a street-level celebration that has grown into one of the city's signature community events. The parade route drew crowds of residents, local organizations, and small businesses flying their Atlanta pride in the open air.

The event, named for the city's original area code, has evolved from a social media hashtag into a full-fledged civic tradition. This year's parade featured community groups, dance troupes, local nonprofits, and small business floats winding through the city.

Photographer Tabius H. McCoy captured the energy of the day for The Atlanta Voice, documenting moments of connection, creativity, and neighborhood pride. The images showed a cross-section of the city's communities turning out for an event that has become less about any single organization and more about the collective identity of living inside the perimeter.

404 Day has also become a rallying point for local businesses, with restaurants, bars, and shops across the city running specials tied to the date. The parade itself is free and open to anyone, reinforcing the grassroots spirit that launched the tradition.
stories.imageAlt: Crowds celebrating at the 404 Day Parade in downtown Atlanta

stories.headline: Atlanta Pride Opens Nominations for 2026 Grand Marshals
stories.summary: The annual call for nominations honors individuals and organizations whose work has strengthened Georgia's LGBTQ+ community. Nominations are open to the public.
stories.body:
Atlanta Pride has opened nominations for its 2026 Grand Marshals, the honorary title given each year to individuals and organizations whose contributions have uplifted Georgia's LGBTQ+ community.

"Serving as a Grand Marshal is a powerful recognition of the tireless advocacy and dedication that moves our community forward," the organization said in announcing the nomination period.

The Grand Marshal selection process is open to public nominations, allowing community members to put forward the names of people and groups they believe have made a meaningful impact. Past honorees have included activists, healthcare providers, educators, and cultural leaders from across metro Atlanta.

Atlanta Pride, one of the oldest and largest Pride organizations in the Southeast, typically holds its main festival and parade in Piedmont Park each October. The Grand Marshal announcement is one of the first major milestones in the lead-up to the annual celebration.

The nomination period signals the start of Pride season planning in Atlanta, a city that has long been considered the capital of LGBTQ+ life in the South. Details on the nomination deadline and selection criteria are available through Atlanta Pride's official channels.

stories.headline: Decatur to Hold Public Hearing on Beacon Hill Historic District
stories.summary: The Decatur City Commission will hear public input Monday evening on whether to designate Beacon Hill as a local historic district, a move that would shape future development in the neighborhood.
stories.body:
The Decatur City Commission will hold a regular meeting at 7:30 p.m. on Monday, April 6, at Decatur City Hall, featuring a public hearing on the proposed Beacon Hill historic district designation.

The hearing gives residents a chance to weigh in on whether Beacon Hill should receive formal local historic district status, which would add design review requirements for renovations and new construction in the neighborhood. Historic district designation is one of the strongest tools available to communities seeking to preserve neighborhood character against redevelopment pressure.

Beacon Hill, a residential neighborhood in Decatur, has seen increasing development interest as property values in the area have risen. Proponents of the designation argue it would protect the neighborhood's architectural heritage and prevent demolitions of historically significant homes. Opponents have raised concerns about restrictions on property owners' ability to modify their own homes.

The meeting will also include a dinner session at 6 p.m. and a work session at 6:45 p.m. before the full commission meeting. The hearing is one of several land-use and preservation discussions playing out across Decatur as the city balances growth with neighborhood identity.

stories.headline: DeKalb DA Warns of Legal Fight If HB 369 Becomes Law
stories.summary: DeKalb County's district attorney is drawing a line on a bill that would reshape prosecutorial authority, promising a court challenge if the governor signs it.
stories.body:
DeKalb County District Attorney Sherry Boston is warning of a legal battle if House Bill 369 is signed into law by Governor Brian Kemp.

The bill, which passed the Georgia General Assembly during the final days of the 2026 legislative session, would give the state new authority to intervene in local prosecution decisions. Critics, including several metro Atlanta district attorneys, argue the legislation undermines the independence of locally elected prosecutors and was designed to target DAs who have exercised discretion on certain categories of cases.

Boston's public warning signals that DeKalb County intends to be at the forefront of any legal challenge. The DA's office has framed the issue as one of local control and constitutional separation of powers, arguing that the bill allows the state to override the judgment of prosecutors chosen by their own communities.

Supporters of HB 369 counter that the legislation creates necessary accountability for prosecutors who decline to enforce state laws. The debate mirrors similar fights playing out in other states where legislatures have moved to rein in progressive prosecutors.

Governor Kemp has not yet indicated whether he will sign the bill. His decision will determine whether the measure becomes law or dies on his desk, with DeKalb County poised to file suit if it advances.

stories.headline: Atlanta Press Club Sets April Debate Series for 2026 Midterm Candidates
stories.summary: The Press Club will host debates for top Georgia candidates ahead of May's primary elections, continuing its role as the state's premier political forum.
stories.body:
The Atlanta Press Club is gearing up to host another round of candidate debates later this month, giving Georgia voters a chance to hear directly from contenders ahead of the May 2026 primary elections.

Journalist Donna Lowry, who moderated the 14th Congressional District debate in February, will return as part of the debate series. The Press Club, headquartered in Midtown, has established itself as the go-to venue for Georgia political debates, drawing candidates from both parties to face off in a format that emphasizes substantive policy discussion over soundbites.

The 2026 midterm cycle has drawn significant national attention to Georgia, with competitive races across multiple congressional districts and statewide offices. The Press Club debates serve as one of the few opportunities for candidates to engage directly with each other in a structured, journalistic setting.

Details on specific debate dates, participating candidates, and viewing options will be announced as the April schedule firms up. The Press Club's debate series has historically been one of the best-attended political events in Atlanta, drawing media, political operatives, and engaged citizens to its Midtown headquarters.

stories.headline: Hawks Enter Make-or-Break Final Week with Playoff Seeding on the Line
stories.summary: Atlanta's final regular-season stretch features matchups that will determine whether the Hawks lock in a favorable playoff position or face a tougher road.
stories.body:
The Atlanta Hawks are heading into the final week of the NBA regular season with their playoff positioning hanging in the balance. After a season of streaky play, these last few games will determine whether the Hawks secure a more favorable seed or face a tougher draw in the postseason.

The Hawks have shown flashes of the talent that made them a playoff contender, but inconsistency has plagued the team throughout the year. The final stretch offers a chance to build momentum heading into the playoffs, or to stumble into the postseason on shaky footing.

State Farm Arena will host some of the most consequential games of the Hawks' season this week, with the home crowd expected to bring energy for what amounts to a playoff atmosphere in the regular season's closing days.

The stakes extend beyond just seeding. A strong finish could give the team confidence heading into a postseason run, while a poor showing could confirm doubts about whether this roster is built for a deep playoff push. For a franchise that has been rebuilding its identity around young talent, this final week is a measuring stick for where the program stands.

stories.headline: Georgia Passes Bill Ensuring Medicaid Covers Sickle Cell Treatment
stories.summary: A bill headed to the governor's desk would guarantee Medicaid coverage for current sickle cell anemia treatments, a win for advocates who rallied at the Capitol.
stories.body:
The Georgia General Assembly has passed a bill aimed at ensuring Medicaid covers the latest treatments for sickle cell anemia, sending it to Governor Brian Kemp's desk after advocates gathered at the state Capitol to push for its passage.

Sickle cell disease disproportionately affects Black Americans, and Georgia has one of the highest rates of the condition in the country. The bill would require the state's Medicaid program to cover currently available treatments, including newer therapies that have shown promise in reducing the painful episodes and hospitalizations that define the disease.

Advocates who traveled to the Capitol to testify in favor of the bill shared personal stories of living with sickle cell or caring for family members with the condition. Their testimony helped frame the legislation not as an abstract policy question but as a matter of basic healthcare access for thousands of Georgians.

The bill's passage came during the final stretch of the 2026 legislative session, which wrapped up last week. If signed by the governor, it would represent one of the session's most significant healthcare wins, expanding coverage for a patient population that has historically faced barriers to accessing the care they need.

Atlanta's medical community, including providers at Grady Memorial Hospital and Emory Healthcare who treat a significant number of sickle cell patients, has been vocal in supporting expanded coverage.
